title: Firing a Client Who Devalued You
dek: When the boundary is finally drawn, it doesn't always feel like triumph. Sometimes it feels like a sudden, terrifying silence in the room, a ringing in your ears, and the slow realization that your body is finally safe from their subtle, persistent extraction.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Anatomy of Extraction


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.238Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You are sitting in the chair across from them, or perhaps you are staring at the glowing rectangle of your phone, reading the text message that just arrived. It is 4:13 PM on a Tuesday. The light in the room is that flat, unforgiving late-afternoon gray that makes everything look slightly exhausted. You read the words again. The devaluation is not a scream; it is a paper cut. It is a subtle undermining of your expertise, a casual dismissal of your time, a renegotiation of the boundary you set three weeks ago. 

Notice what happens in your body before your brain even formulates a response. Your masseter muscles - the thick, powerful muscles of your jaw - clench so tightly that your teeth ache. The fascia across your chest tightens, pulling your shoulders forward in a microscopic cringe. Your breath stops at the top of your diaphragm, refusing to descend into your belly. This is not a psychological metaphor. This is biology. Your body is registering a threat. It is registering the presence of someone who is consuming you without your consent.

We are taught, especially as women, to override this somatic alarm bell. We are taught to soften our faces, to take a deep breath, to find the "win-win" solution, to be professional, to be accommodating. We are taught that our anger is a moral failure, a sign that we are not evolved enough, not spiritual enough, not compassionate enough. But your body knows the truth. Your body knows that this client is extracting from you. 

Gabor Maté writes extensively about the physiological cost of this kind of chronic suppression. He notes that when we chronically repress our authentic "no" in order to maintain an attachment - even a professional one - we turn that aggressive energy inward. The immune system becomes confused. The nervous system remains in a state of low-grade sympathetic arousal. You are not just annoyed; you are being biologically depleted. The exhaustion you feel after interacting with this client is not because you are weak. It is because your body is working overtime to manage the dissonance between what you know to be true and what you are forcing yourself to tolerate.

## The Taste of Copper

When you finally decide to fire them, the decision rarely arrives like a bolt of lightning. It usually arrives like a slow, sickening realization. It tastes like copper in the back of your throat. It smells like stale sweat - the sharp, acrid sweat of anxiety, not the clean sweat of exertion. You might wake up at 3:00 AM, the hour when the liver meridian is most active in Traditional Chinese Medicine, your heart hammering against your ribs, your mind racing through all the ways you could have handled the situation better. 

In TCM, anger is associated with the liver. When the liver qi is stagnant, when the anger is not allowed to move and express itself, it creates heat. It creates tension. It creates the exact kind of jaw-clenching, shoulder-hiking, breath-holding misery you are experiencing right now. The herbs we use to move liver qi - like bupleurum or dandelion root - are bitter. They are not sweet. They do not coddle you. They demand that the energy move. 

You must become like the bitter herb. You must demand that the energy move. 

Karla McLaren, in her groundbreaking work on emotions, describes anger as the emotion that sets boundaries. Anger is the honorable sentry standing at the edge of your personal territory, holding a spear, saying, "No further." When a client devalues you, they are crossing that boundary. They are stepping into your territory and tracking mud across the floor. Your anger is the appropriate, necessary, and deeply healthy response to that intrusion. To suppress the anger is to fire the sentry and leave the gates wide open.

## The Mechanics of the Severing

How do you actually do it? How do you fire the client? You do not do it with a long, explanatory email detailing all the ways they have hurt you. You do not do it by defending your worth. Defending your worth implies that your worth is up for debate. It is not.

You do it with a clean, sharp edge. 

"Dear [Name], I am writing to let you know that I will no longer be able to provide services for you, effective immediately. I wish you the best in finding a practitioner who is a better fit for your needs."

That is it. That is the whole message. 

When you hit send, your body will likely go into a state of shock. The adrenaline will spike. Your hands might shake. You might feel a sudden, overwhelming urge to vomit. This is normal. You have just performed an act of self-preservation that goes against decades of conditioning. You have just prioritized your own somatic integrity over someone else's comfort. 

Bessel van der Kolk reminds us that the body keeps the score. The body remembers every time you swallowed your truth to keep the peace. But the body also remembers the moment you finally stood up for yourself. The body remembers the exact sensation of your finger clicking the "send" button. It remembers the sudden, terrifying silence that follows. 

## The Aftermath and the Echo

The silence is the hardest part. After the client is fired, there is a void where their demands used to be. You might feel a strange sense of grief. You might doubt yourself. You might wonder if you were too harsh, if you misunderstood them, if you are simply too sensitive. 

This is the echo of the devaluation. The client is gone, but their voice is still rattling around in your head. 

This is when you must turn to your somatic practices. You must ground yourself in the physical reality of your body, right here, right now. You must remind your nervous system that the threat is gone.

### A Short Embodied Practice for the Aftermath

Find a place to stand where you will not be interrupted. Take off your shoes. Feel the floor beneath your feet. Notice the texture of the floor - is it wood, carpet, tile? Is it cold or warm? 

Bring your attention to your psoas muscles, the deep core muscles that connect your spine to your legs. These are the muscles that contract when we are in a fetal position, when we are protecting our vital organs. They are likely tight right now. 

Place your hands on your lower belly. Take a breath that actually moves your hands outward. Do not force it; just invite the breath to descend. 

Now, I want you to physically shake. Start with your hands. Shake them as if you are trying to flick water off your fingers. Let the shaking move up your arms, into your shoulders. Let your jaw hang slack. Shake your right leg, then your left leg. Shake your whole body. Do this for two full minutes. 

Animals do this in the wild after they have survived a threat. They shake off the excess adrenaline. They reset their nervous systems. You are an animal. Shake it off. 

When you stop, stand completely still. Notice the tingling in your skin. Notice the temperature of your body. Notice the space around you. You are here. You are safe. The boundary holds.

## The Medicine of Refusal

Firing a client who devalues you is not just a business decision. It is a profound act of self-respect. It is a declaration that your energy, your time, and your expertise are sacred. 

In my practice, I often recommend [Gaia Herbs SleepThru (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004GJYTF8?tag=spankyspinola-20) for clients who are recovering from this kind of chronic boundary violation. Ashwagandha is an adaptogen; it helps the body manage stress and rebuild resilience. But no herb can do the work of a boundary. The herb can only support the body that has already decided to protect itself. 

Similarly, reading [Gaia Herbs Calm A.S.A.P.](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07H2QFMNR?tag=spankyspinola-20) can give you the intellectual framework to understand why the devaluation hurts so much, but the intellectual framework is not the cure. The cure is the action. The cure is the refusal.

When you refuse to be devalued, you are not just healing yourself. You are changing the energetic ecosystem around you. You are signaling to the world, and to your own nervous system, that you are no longer available for extraction. 


When the adrenaline is still coursing through your veins hours after the email is sent, you need a physical intervention. I often use an [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54H9G?tag=spankyspinola-20) to force my nervous system to recalibrate. The sharp plastic spikes demand the body's full attention, pulling you out of the looping thoughts in your prefrontal cortex and slamming you back into the raw, physical present. It hurts, and then it releases. It is the exact somatic mirror of the boundary you just set.

## The Space That Opens Up

What happens next? What happens after the shaking stops, after the adrenaline fades, after the client is truly gone? 

Space opens up. 

At first, the space might feel terrifying. It might feel like a loss of income, a loss of status, a loss of connection. But nature abhors a vacuum. When you remove the toxic element from your ecosystem, you create room for something new to grow. 

You might notice that your sleep improves. You might notice that the chronic pain in your levator scapulae - the muscle that hikes your shoulder up to your ear - begins to soften. You might notice that you have more energy for the clients who actually respect you, who actually value your work. 

This is the reward of the boundary. It is not a parade. It is not a medal. It is the quiet, steady return of your own vitality. It is the feeling of your breath moving freely through your diaphragm. It is the feeling of your jaw relaxing. It is the feeling of being at home in your own body.

## The Ongoing Practice

Do not expect to be perfect at this. You will likely tolerate devaluation again in the future. You will likely override your somatic alarm bells again. The conditioning is deep. The fear of being "unprofessional" or "difficult" is strong. 

But every time you set the boundary, every time you fire the client, every time you choose your own somatic integrity over someone else's comfort, you are building a new neural pathway. You are strengthening the muscle of refusal. 

And the next time it happens, the alarm bell will ring a little louder. The taste of copper will be a little sharper. The decision will come a little faster. 

You are learning to trust your body. You are learning to trust the sentry at the gate. You are learning that your anger is not a monster to be tamed, but a messenger to be heeded. 

Listen to the messenger. Fire the client. Reclaim your territory. 


## The Somatic Cost of "Being the Bigger Person"

We are constantly told to "be the bigger person." This phrase is a weapon disguised as wisdom. When a client devalues you - when they question your rates, when they ignore your advice but blame you for the lack of results, when they treat your time as their personal playground - being the "bigger person" usually means swallowing your very valid anger. 

What happens to that swallowed anger? It does not evaporate. It sinks into the tissues. It lodges in the trapezius muscles, turning them into hard, aching knots. It settles in the gut, disrupting your digestion, creating a low-level nausea that you try to ignore with ginger tea and deep breathing. But ginger tea cannot digest the disrespect you have swallowed. 

Resmaa Menakem teaches us about the concept of "clean pain" versus "dirty pain." Clean pain is the pain of healing, the pain of setting a boundary, the pain of stepping into the unknown. It hurts, but it moves you forward. Dirty pain is the pain of avoidance, the pain of staying in a toxic dynamic, the pain of betraying yourself to keep the peace. 

When you refuse to fire the client who devalues you, you are choosing dirty pain. You are choosing the chronic, grinding ache of self-betrayal. When you finally send that email, when you finally sever the cord, you are choosing clean pain. The adrenaline spike, the shaking hands, the temporary guilt - that is clean pain. It is the pain of a wound being cleaned out so it can finally heal.

## The Illusion of the "Difficult" Client

There is a pervasive myth in the wellness and service industries that there are no bad clients, only practitioners who lack the skill to handle them. This is a dangerous, gaslighting lie. It is a lie that keeps practitioners - especially women - trapped in cycles of abuse and extraction. 

Some people are simply committed to their own dysfunction. Some people use practitioners not to heal, but to have an audience for their suffering. Some people use the dynamic of paying for a service as an excuse to enact their unhealed power struggles. 

When you recognize this, you must stop trying to "fix" the dynamic. You cannot out-therapize, out-coach, or out-heal someone's commitment to devaluing you. Your empathy is a beautiful tool, but it is not a magic wand that can override someone else's free will. 

Notice the physical sensation of trying to help someone who is actively resisting you. It feels like pushing a boulder up a hill covered in mud. Your lower back aches. Your hamstrings tighten. You feel a deep, bone-weary exhaustion that sleep does not cure. This is your body telling you that the effort is futile. 

Listen to the exhaustion. It is a profound teacher. It is telling you to put the boulder down. 

## The Ritual of Reclaiming

After the client is gone, you need a ritual to clear their energy from your space and your nervous system. This is not about "love and light" or spiritual bypassing. This is about somatic hygiene. 

Just as you would wash your hands after touching something contaminated, you must wash your nervous system after interacting with someone who devalues you. 

Run a bath. Make it as hot as you can stand. Add two cups of Epsom salts and a handful of dried mugwort. Mugwort is a fierce, protective herb. It is the herb of boundaries and psychic clearing. As you soak in the water, visualize the energetic cords connecting you to that client dissolving. 

Feel the heat of the water penetrating your muscles. Notice where you are still holding tension. Is your jaw still tight? Are your shoulders still creeping up toward your ears? Consciously invite those muscles to release. 

When you drain the tub, watch the water go down the drain. Imagine all the residual frustration, all the swallowed anger, all the dirty pain going down the drain with it. You are clean. You are clear. You are yours again.

## The Return of the Sentry

As you practice this - as you get better at recognizing devaluation and swifter at severing the connection - you will notice a shift in your posture. You will literally stand taller. The chronic slump of the accommodating woman will begin to correct itself. 

Your anger, once a terrifying force that you tried to suppress, will become a trusted ally. It will become the sentry at the gate, standing tall, holding the spear, keeping watch. 

You will no longer fear the sentry. You will welcome her. You will say, "Thank you for letting me know that someone is tracking mud on the floor. I will handle it." 

And you will handle it. With a clean, sharp edge. With a steady voice. With a body that is grounded, present, and fiercely protective of its own sacred territory. 

This is the work. This is the practice. This is how we heal the world - not by being endlessly accommodating, but by being uncompromisingly true to our own somatic integrity. 

## The Architecture of the Boundary

A boundary is not a wall. A wall is static, defensive, and built out of fear. A boundary is a living, breathing membrane. It is the cell wall that lets nutrients in and keeps toxins out. It requires constant somatic awareness to maintain. 

When you are dealing with a client who devalues you, they are constantly testing the permeability of that membrane. They send an email at 11:00 PM and expect an immediate response. They ask for "just one more quick thing" that takes two hours. They subtly critique your methods while demanding more of your time. 

Your body registers every single one of these micro-intrusions. The slight tightening of the throat. The flutter of anxiety in the solar plexus. The sudden, inexplicable urge to eat sugar or scroll mindlessly on your phone. These are all somatic strategies to manage the discomfort of a boundary being breached. 

Instead of numbing the discomfort, lean into it. Let the discomfort be the alarm bell. 

When the 11:00 PM email arrives, do not reply. Notice the urge to reply. Notice the fear that if you don't reply, they will be angry, they will leave, you will fail. Sit with that fear. Breathe into the solar plexus. Remind your body: "I am safe. I am allowed to rest. My time belongs to me." 

This is the micro-practice that prepares you for the macro-action of firing them. You are building the somatic tolerance for their displeasure. You are learning that you can survive their disappointment, their anger, their devaluation. You do not need their approval to exist.

## The Physiology of the "Yes" When You Mean "No"

Let us examine the exact physiological sequence of saying "yes" when your entire organism is screaming "no." 

The client asks for a discount, or a schedule change that ruins your day off, or an exception to your clearly stated policies. The request lands in your inbox. 

Instantly, the amygdala - the primitive alarm bell in your brain - fires. It recognizes the request not as a simple question, but as a threat to your resources, your time, and your autonomy. The sympathetic nervous system kicks into gear. Cortisol and adrenaline flood your bloodstream. Your heart rate variability drops. Your pupils dilate slightly. Blood moves away from your digestive organs and toward your extremities, preparing you to fight or flee. 

But you do neither. You sit at your desk. You stare at the screen. 

Your prefrontal cortex, heavily conditioned by decades of socialization, steps in. It says, "If I say no, they will be mad. If they are mad, I am unsafe. I must appease them to restore safety." 

So, you type the "yes." You hit send. 

What happens to all that mobilized survival energy? It has nowhere to go. You didn't fight. You didn't flee. You froze, and then you fawned. The energy gets trapped in the nervous system. It becomes the chronic tension in your neck. It becomes the mysterious digestive issue. It becomes the sudden, inexplicable wave of exhaustion that hits you at 2:00 PM. 

This is the cost of the forced "yes." It is a biological tax levied on your own body. 

When you finally decide to fire the client, you are refusing to pay the tax anymore. You are allowing the fight response to complete its cycle. The anger you feel is the fight response waking up from its frozen state. It is the life force returning to your limbs. 

Do not suppress it. Let the anger move. Let it give you the courage to type the "no." Let it give you the strength to sever the tie. 

## The Myth of the "Good" Practitioner

We must dismantle the myth of the "good" practitioner. The good practitioner is endlessly available. The good practitioner never gets angry. The good practitioner can heal anyone, tolerate anything, and turn every toxic dynamic into a beautiful story of transformation. 

This myth is a cage. It is a cage built by a culture that expects women to be infinite resources of care, with no needs, no limits, and no boundaries of their own. 

When you fire a client who devalues you, you are stepping out of the cage. You are declaring that you are not an infinite resource. You are a human animal with limits, needs, and a finite amount of energy. 

You are declaring that your work is valuable, and that it requires a reciprocal exchange of respect. If the respect is not there, the work cannot happen. 

This is not a failure. This is a profound success. It is the success of a practitioner who has finally learned to value herself as much as she values her clients. 

It is the success of a woman who has finally learned to listen to her own body. 

## The Somatic Resonance of Truth

When you speak the truth - when you say, "This is not working for me, and I am ending our professional relationship" - there is a specific somatic resonance that occurs. 

It feels like a tuning fork being struck inside your chest. It is a clear, vibrating note of alignment. 

For so long, you have been living in dissonance. The client devalues you, your body says "danger," but your words say "no problem." This dissonance creates a buzzing, chaotic static in your nervous system. 

The moment you align your words with your somatic reality, the static clears. The tuning fork rings true. 

You might still feel afraid. You might still feel the adrenaline. But beneath the fear, there is a deep, unshakable solidity. You are standing on the bedrock of your own truth. 

This solidity is what allows you to weather the client's reaction. Whether they are angry, defensive, or dismissive, their reaction no longer has the power to shatter you. You are anchored in the truth of your own experience. 

You know what you felt. You know what they did. You know what you need to do to protect yourself. 

No one can take that knowing away from you. 

## The Harvest of the Boundary

What do you harvest from this difficult, bitter work? 

You harvest yourself. 

You gather up all the pieces of your energy, your attention, and your life force that you had scattered at the feet of people who did not value them. You call your power back into your own body. 

You become whole again. 

And from this place of wholeness, your work transforms. You are no longer operating from a place of depletion and resentment. You are operating from a place of overflow. 

The clients who find you now will feel the difference. They will feel the solidity of your boundaries. They will feel the clarity of your presence. They will respect you, because you have finally learned to respect yourself. 

This is the ultimate medicine. This is the alchemy of anger turned into boundaries, of devaluation turned into self-respect, of extraction turned into wholeness. 

Drink the bitter medicine. Fire the client. Reclaim your life.

## The Deep Fascia of Resentment

Let us look deeper into the physical structures of resentment. Resentment is not just a feeling; it is a physical accumulation. It is the slow calcification of the fascia, the connective tissue that wraps around every muscle, bone, and organ in your body. When you repeatedly allow a client to devalue you, the fascia responds by thickening, hardening, and restricting movement. It is trying to build a physical armor because you have refused to build a relational one. 

You might notice this as a chronic stiffness in your neck that no amount of stretching can resolve. You might feel it as a tightness in your chest that makes taking a full, deep breath feel impossible. This is the fascia holding the memory of every time you smiled when you wanted to scream, every time you said "it's fine" when it was absolutely not fine. 

Pema Chödrön speaks of the "shenpa," the hook, the urge, the habitual tendency to close down when we are triggered. The client's devaluation is the hook. Your habitual response - to appease, to shrink, to swallow the anger - is the closing down. The fascia physically manifests this closing down. 

To release the fascia, you must stop the behavior that is causing it to harden. You must unhook yourself. Firing the client is the ultimate unhooking. It is the moment you refuse to take the bait. 

When you do this, the fascia can finally begin to hydrate, to soften, to glide again. The armor is no longer needed because the threat has been removed. The physical relief is often immediate and profound. You might find yourself taking the first full, unrestricted breath you have taken in months. 

This is the somatic reward of the boundary. It is the return of fluidity, of movement, of breath. It is the body thanking you for finally doing your job as its protector.

## The Medicine of the Bitter Truth

We have been sold a toxic version of healing that insists everything must be sweet, gentle, and harmonious. But true healing often requires the bitter truth. 

In herbalism, we use bitter herbs to stimulate digestion, to wake up the liver, to clear stagnation. The bitter taste is a shock to the system. It says, "Wake up. Pay attention. Something needs to move." 

Firing a client is the bitter medicine. It is not pleasant. It does not taste good. But it is absolutely necessary for the health of your practice and the health of your body. 

When you finally deliver the bitter medicine - when you send the email, when you make the call, when you draw the line - you are acting as your own healer. You are prescribing the exact remedy your body has been begging for. 

And just like after taking a dose of strong bitter herbs, you might feel a temporary wave of nausea or discomfort. But then, the digestion begins. The stagnation clears. The energy moves. 

You will feel a profound sense of relief. You will feel the return of your own power. You will realize that the client who devalued you was not a reflection of your worth, but a test of your boundaries. 

You passed the test. You held the line. You protected the sacred territory of your own body and your own work. 

And that, my friend, is the most profound healing of all.

## FAQ

**Q: What if I need the money? I can't afford to fire this client.**
**A:** This is the most common and most painful reality. If you cannot fire them immediately because of financial survival, you must change your internal relationship to the work. You must consciously acknowledge to yourself: "I am choosing to endure this extraction temporarily for money, but I do not agree with it, and I am actively planning my exit." Do not gaslight yourself into thinking the devaluation is acceptable. Protect your core. Use somatic practices to discharge the stress daily. And start building the bridge out.

**Q: What if they get angry and badmouth me to others?**
**A:** They might. People who are used to extracting without consequence often throw tantrums when a boundary is set. This is the extinction burst. Let them have their tantrum. Your reputation is built on the quality of your work and the integrity of your boundaries, not on the approval of people who devalue you. The clients who are meant for you will not be deterred by the complaints of someone who wanted to consume you for free.

**Q: I feel guilty for firing them. Does that mean I made the wrong choice?**
**A:** No. Guilt is often just the conditioned response to breaking a rule. You broke the rule that says women must always be accommodating, always be nice, always put others first. The guilt is not a signal that you did something wrong; it is a signal that you did something new. Let the guilt be there. Notice where it lives in your body. Breathe into it. It will pass. The relief that follows will be permanent.

**Q: Should I explain to them exactly why I'm firing them so they can learn and grow?**
**A:** Absolutely not. It is not your job to be their therapist, their mother, or their spiritual guide. Explaining yourself is a trap; it invites debate, defensiveness, and further extraction. They are not paying you to teach them how to be decent humans. A clean, professional severing is the most compassionate thing you can do for both of you. It leaves no room for argument and preserves your energy for the people who actually want to do the work.

---
